blocks.text:
Michael Martin
Chief Operating Officer
Coleman Ltd
Dear Mr. Martin,

This letter confirms that you have retained Northgate Talent Partners to conduct a search for a Vice President of Engineering, and sets out the terms on which we will do the work. I have written it the way I would want it written for me: short where it can be, precise where it has to be. If any of it does not match what we agreed on the phone, say so before you sign rather than after. A search that begins with two different understandings of the assignment ends badly, and it ends badly late.
Scope of the Engagement

We will conduct a retained search for a Vice President of Engineering reporting to you. The engagement is with {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}} and commences on {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}}. It is exclusive for its duration. We will not present candidates for this role on behalf of any other party, and we ask in return that candidates reaching you through other channels, whether a referral from a director or someone who applies to you directly, be routed to us before you engage with them. That is not a fee protection point. It is that one team has to hold every conversation, or the market hears two versions of your story and believes the worse one.
How We Will Work

The opening weeks are specification and mapping. We will interview you and the people the role touches, agree a written position specification, and build a map of the companies where this profile actually lives. Nothing is sourced against a document you have not signed. We then bring you a small set of anonymized profiles for calibration, which exists so that we can be wrong early and cheaply about what you want, rather than late and expensively. Approaches and first conversations follow. When we have a slate we can defend, you meet it. References come after you have met people, not before, because a reference call is a favor spent and we do not spend it on a candidate you would not hire. We stay in it through the offer and through the resignation the candidate has to give, which is the part clients most often underestimate.
Your Team

Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant, leads the day-to-day work and writes the position specification and the market map. James Grant, Senior Consultant, carries the client and candidate relationships; he is the one who will call you when there is something to say that should not wait for a written report, and he is the one who will keep a nervous finalist at the table. John Chang, Research Associate, builds and maintains the sourcing list. I remain accountable for the engagement. If it drifts, you will hear that from me, and you will hear it in the second sentence.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for this engagement is {{fact:f:E-2016-001.fee}}. It is fixed. It does not move with the compensation of the person you hire, and it does not move if the search runs long. We invoice it in three equal installments: the first on commencement, the second thirty days after, the third sixty days after. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. Reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, meaning candidate travel we arrange at your request and our own travel beyond the local market, are billed at cost against receipts. We will not incur any single expense above a threshold you set at kickoff without asking you first.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}} until the search concludes, meaning the person we place starts work or you tell us the role is closed. Either of us may terminate on fifteen days' written notice. If you terminate, you owe the installments invoiced to that date and nothing further; we will not bill work in progress against a search you have stopped. If we terminate, which happens when we conclude we cannot serve the assignment honestly, we refund any installment covering work we did not do. For twelve months following a placement, we will not approach the person we place, or any employee of Coleman Ltd, on behalf of another client.
Confidentiality

This runs both directions. What you tell us about the company, the team, the reason the seat is open, and what you pay is confidential and stays inside the engagement team named above. In the other direction, candidate information is confidential to you and to the people you name as part of the process. This matters more in search than clients expect it to. Most of the people worth talking to are employed, are doing well, and take a real risk by taking our call. We describe candidates by their current role rather than by name in anything that circulates, and we ask you to hold candidate material to the same standard. Please tell us at kickoff who inside the company will see it, and tell us again if that list grows.
Limitation of Liability

We will conduct the search with the care and skill of a professional firm, and we will verify what candidates tell us about their history to the extent references and the public record allow. We cannot warrant a hire. The decision to make an offer is yours, and the performance of the person you hire is a matter of your management as much as of our selection. Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it, and neither of us is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. If the person we place resigns or is terminated for cause within twelve months of starting, we will conduct one replacement search at no additional fee, expenses excepted. That replacement search is your sole remedy on the placement.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the laws of the state in which the firm maintains its principal office, and both of us submit to the courts of that state for any dispute arising out of it. If a dispute does arise, we would rather sit down with you first. Neither of us will file anything until we have tried that in a room, once, in person.

If this reflects our agreement, please sign below and return one copy for our file. Jeffrey Patterson will be in touch within the first week to schedule the intake interviews. Thank you for the trust. We do not take a retained assignment we do not believe we can complete, and we believe we can complete this one.

To: James Grant, John Chang
From: Jeffrey Patterson
Re: VP Engineering search, working plan

The engagement letter is signed and the search for {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}} commences on {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}}. This memo is the plan of record. The objective, stated once so that we can be held to it: place a Vice President of Engineering at Coleman Ltd who can run the engineering organization through the next stage of its growth and who is still there in three years. Michael Martin, their Chief Operating Officer, is the hiring executive and our single point of contact. The role reports to him.

Two things about this assignment that I want on the record at the start, because they will be less comfortable to say in week ten. First, we do not yet know what the client wants. We know what the job description says, which is not the same thing, and the intake interviews exist to find the distance between the two. Second, this profile is not deep in this market. I would rather write that sentence now than discover it halfway through the slate.
Workstreams

items: Intake and specification. Owner: me. Interviews with Michael Martin and with everyone the role touches, then a written position specification back to the client for signature. Nothing gets sourced against an unsigned specification, including by me.

Market map. Owner: John Chang, with me. Target company list first, name list second. John, build the company list before you build the name list. I want us arguing about which companies are in bounds before we are arguing about which people are.

Client relationship. Owner: James Grant. Jim owns the temperature reading, which is the thing no document will tell us: whether they will actually hire, who else has a vote we have not been told about, and what the internal candidate situation is. There is always an internal candidate situation.
Candidate development. Owners: James Grant and me. Jim makes the first call to anyone senior; the people we want are employed and will take a call from a person before they will take one from a firm.

Records and discretion. Owner: John Chang. Candidates go into the file and into anything that circulates by current role, not by name, from the first entry rather than from the first time it matters. This is easy to do from the start and impossible to retrofit.

Weekly written update to the client. Owner: me, with Jim's read attached to it. It goes every week whether or not there is news, because a week of silence from a retained firm is read as a week of nothing happening.

blocks.text: On sequencing: the calibration session is the cheapest thing we will do and the most valuable. Its whole purpose is to let the client be wrong in front of us, early, about a profile that costs nothing, rather than in front of a candidate, late, about a profile that costs the assignment. Do not let it get postponed for a busy week. If it slips twice, that is information about the client and I want to hear about it.
Next Steps

items: Intake interviews with Michael Martin inside the first week. I will send times today.
Jim to get us the reporting chart and the compensation band, and to find out who at Coleman Ltd besides our contact has a say in this.
John to have the target company list in front of me by the end of week two.
Draft position specification to the client at the end of week three.
Standing team call weekly. Fifteen minutes when there is nothing, and longer when there is.

blocks.text: One note on tone. Everything we send this client is also a sample of how we work, and they have no other sample yet. Reread it before it goes.
Michael, copying James Grant and John Chang.

The specification came back from your side with two changes, both of which I think are right, and it is now the document we are sourcing against. John Chang has the target company list closed and is most of the way through turning it into a name list. None of it is worth showing you yet, but the shape is already saying something and I would rather say it now than at the slate: the profile you described lives in a narrow band of companies, and a few of those are places we cannot approach on your behalf. Not a problem today. It becomes one if the narrow band turns out to be the only band.

Two asks. First, thirty minutes with whoever runs product for you, before the end of next week. The specification says this role partners with product and I would rather hear that from the other side of the partnership than take it on faith. James Grant is with your team shortly and can pick the scheduling up in person. Second, tell us who else inside the company has a vote on this hire. If there is a name we do not have, we would rather have it now than meet it at the slate review.
I am keeping the working file under {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}, VP Engineering. If you send us something that should not sit in that file, say so in the note and it will not.
Jeffrey Patterson

This message and anything attached to it are intended for Michael Martin and the recipients named above at Coleman Ltd, and contain confidential search material. If it has reached you in error, please delete it and tell me.
Vice President of Engineering Search: Coleman Ltd

items: Client: {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2016-001.start}}
Prepared for Michael Martin, Chief Operating Officer
Prepared by Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant
Confidential. Candidates are identified by current role only.

blocks.text: Where the Search Stands

items: Specification agreed and signed. We are sourcing against it, not against the job description it started as.
Target company list closed at the companies we can approach and will approach.
Market map complete. Long list built and being worked.
First conversations underway. No slate yet, and none should be expected at this point in the work.
On plan.

blocks.text: What the Market Looks Like

items: The profile is real but thin. The population that has done this job at this scale is smaller than the specification implies it is.
Nearly all of it is employed, senior, and not looking. Every conversation opens as a favor, not as an application.
Two adjacent pools are worth opening: operators one level down inside larger organizations, and this profile at companies going through a different kind of change.
Compensation is not the binding constraint we expected. Scope is.

blocks.text: How We Are Working It

items: Approaches are made by a person, not by a firm. James Grant makes the first call at the senior end, because that is the call that gets returned.
John Chang holds the map and the record. Every approach, every response, every decline with its reason.
Nothing goes forward that we have not met twice. You should not be the second interview a candidate has had.
We keep the set-asides. What we put down and why is as much of the product as what we bring you.

blocks.text: What We Need From You

items: A calibration session on four to six anonymized profiles. These are samples, not candidates; nobody in the pack is someone you can hire.
A decision on the adjacent pools above. In or out, decided once, so that we are not relitigating it at the slate.
Confirmation of everyone who holds a vote on this hire, including anyone who holds one informally.
Interview availability blocked now, ahead of the slate rather than after it.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: James Grant runs the calibration session with you and your team.
John Chang extends the map into the adjacent pools, should you open them.
Jeffrey Patterson reissues the specification if calibration moves it, and it usually moves it.
Written update weekly. James Grant calls if something cannot wait for the written one.

blocks.text: Working session on the Vice President of Engineering search for {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}, held at the client's offices. Minutes recorded by John Chang. Circulated to attendees for correction; corrections to me within two business days, after which these stand as the record.
Attendees

items: Michael Martin, Chief Operating Officer, Coleman Ltd
Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant
James Grant, Senior Consultant
John Chang, Research Associate (recording)

blocks.text: Discussion

The purpose of the session was to review progress against the position specification and to agree how the slate will be composed before candidate interviews begin. Jeffrey Patterson opened with the state of the long list and the outcome of the calibration session.

Jeffrey Patterson reported that the calibration profiles moved the specification in one material respect. The requirement for direct experience at the current scale has been relaxed in favor of demonstrated experience taking an organization through a scale transition. Michael Martin confirmed this reflected the discussion on his side and asked that the change be written into the specification rather than carried informally between the two teams. Jeffrey Patterson agreed and said the reissued specification would follow, with the changed paragraphs marked so that nobody has to read the whole document twice.

James Grant raised the internal candidate. Michael Martin confirmed that a member of the existing engineering leadership has expressed interest and will be considered. James Grant asked whether that person would be assessed on the same basis as the external slate, by the same people, and Michael Martin said yes. James Grant asked that the answer be given to the individual directly and early, and made the point that an internal candidate who learns their position from the process rather than from their executive becomes a retention problem regardless of how the search ends. Michael Martin accepted this and will speak to the person concerned.

The session returned to the adjacent pools opened at calibration. John Chang reported that the extended map has produced a workable number of additional approaches, and that the response rate out of the adjacent pool is running higher than out of the core pool. Jeffrey Patterson attributed the difference to the core pool being well paid and well treated where it already sits. Michael Martin asked whether the firm was reaching outside the specification in order to fill a slate. Jeffrey Patterson said it was not, and offered to bring the set-aside profiles as well as the advancing ones to the slate review, so that the client can see what was put down and why.

On slate timing, James Grant advised against assembling a slate to meet a date. Michael Martin said the board asks about this role monthly and that he would rather answer honestly than early. The session agreed that the slate goes forward when it is ready, and that the weekly update will say so plainly if it slips.

On logistics, Michael Martin will block interview availability in advance. James Grant will schedule with candidates directly rather than through the client's calendar, for discretion. Candidate material circulating at Coleman Ltd will identify candidates by current role until finalists are agreed.
Action Items

rows: Reissue the position specification with the scale-transition change marked
Speak to the internal candidate about the process and the basis of assessment
Extend the market map into the adjacent pool and report response rates
Confirm the interview panel and block availability
Circulate the slate review agenda, including the set-aside profiles

blocks.text: Vice President of Engineering search for {{fact:f:E-2016-001.client}}. Prepared by Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant. Confidential to Coleman Ltd and to the engagement team.
Status Summary

At the midpoint of the engagement the search is on plan. The position specification is signed in its reissued form, the market map is complete and has been extended once at your instruction, the long list has been worked down through first conversations, and a slate is forming. It is not ready. I want to be precise about that rather than optimistic. I expect to bring you candidates, I expect it to be soon, and I am not going to put a date in a document that will outlive my memory of writing it. This is the stage of a retained search where a great deal of work sits behind us and very little of it is visible to you, which is uncomfortable for both of us and is about to end.
Accomplishments This Period

items: The specification was reissued after the working session, with the scale requirement rewritten as a transition requirement, and signed. Everything sourced since has been sourced against that version.

John Chang completed the extension of the map into the adjacent pool your team opened at calibration. The extension has earned its keep: the response rate out of the adjacent pool is materially better than out of the core, for exactly the reason we predicted, which is that the core is well paid and well regarded where it already sits and has no reason to answer the phone.

First conversations are complete across the core group and substantially complete across the extension. James Grant has taken the senior end of these himself, which is the only way that end gets taken.

We have set aside more profiles than we have advanced, and we have kept the reasons in writing. You will see the set-asides at the slate review alongside the advances. A slate whose shape you cannot see is a slate you cannot argue with, and a slate you cannot argue with is one you will not defend to your board.

blocks.text: Upcoming Work

items: Second conversations with the advancing group, run by James Grant and me together, so that two people here have met every candidate before anyone there meets one.
Written candidate reports on the advancing group, by current role rather than by name until you confirm the panel.
On-list references on the advancing group. Off-list references come after you have met people, not before.
Slate review with your panel, in the format agreed at the working session.

blocks.text: Risks

The thin market remains the binding constraint, as we told you in the briefing. Opening the adjacent pool has widened it enough to work with, but not enough that we can afford to lose two people late. That is the argument for moving quickly once you have a slate rather than for moving quickly to get one.

The internal candidate is a live risk in both directions, and it is not a risk to the search so much as a risk to your engineering organization. We have taken you at your word that the assessment is on the same basis. Hold to that even if the external slate is strong, because the part of this the rest of your team will remember is not who was hired but whether the process was honest.

Timing is against us for the next stretch. This is the worst window in the calendar for both halves of the problem, since candidates take holiday and so do panels, and a two-week gap between conversations is how good candidates cool. James Grant will manage the gaps on the candidate side. The panel side is yours.

Two of the people we are advancing are in another process. This is not a failure of confidentiality and it is not a surprise; it is what happens when you insist on people who are worth insisting on. It is a reason to have your interview availability blocked before the slate arrives rather than after. A competing process is rarely won on money. It is usually won by whichever company made the candidate feel that a decision was being taken about them rather than around them, and speed is most of how that gets communicated.
Fees and Budget

The engagement is on a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2016-001.fee}}. The fee does not move with the length of the search or with the compensation of the person you hire, and all three installments have now been invoiced under the schedule in the engagement letter. Out-of-pocket expenses to date are limited to candidate travel arranged at your request and billed at cost against receipts. There is no budget variance to report, and there will not be one. The only number that changes between here and the close is the calendar.
Written reporting continues weekly. James Grant will call if something arrives that should not wait for a written report, which in my experience it does about once a search.
